description: Figure 16 presents a multi-axis line graph showing the variation of tensile properties of Fe–10Mn–10Al–0.7C low-density steel as a function of annealing temperature (700–1200 °C) for 1 h, with the hot-rolled (HR) state included for comparison. The left y-axis reports strength in MPa, while the right y-axes display elongation to failure (%) and the product of strength and ductility (GPa%). Four data series are plotted: tensile strength (red squares), yield strength (magenta diamonds), ultimate elongation (green circles), and the product of strength and ductility (blue stars). Both tensile and yield strengths decrease monotonically with increasing annealing temperature, dropping from approximately 1100 MPa and 1000 MPa in the HR condition to about 611 MPa and 495 MPa at 1200 °C. In contrast, ultimate elongation initially rises from ~5% at 700 °C to a peak of ~36.6% at 900 °C, then declines sharply at higher temperatures. The product of strength and ductility follows a similar trend to elongation, reaching a maximum of approximately 31 GPa% at 900 °C. This figure supports the conclusion that annealing at 900 °C for 1 h provides the optimal balance of strength and ductility, which is closely related to κ-carbide dissolution and grain growth behavior.
